chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: If you feed a large language model the phrase, Michael Jordan plays the sport of blank,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and you have it predict what comes next, and it correctly predicts basketball,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: this would suggest that somewhere inside its hundreds of billions of parameters,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: it's baked in knowledge about a specific person and his specific sport. And I think in general,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: anyone who's played around with one of these models has the clear sense that it's memorized

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: tons and tons of facts. So a reasonable question you could ask is, how exactly does that work?

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And where do those facts live?

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Last December, a few researchers from Google DeepMind posted about work on this question,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and they were using this specific example of matching athletes to their sports.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And although a full mechanistic understanding of how facts are stored remains unsolved,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: they had some interesting partial results, including the very general high-level conclusion

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: that the facts seem to live inside a specific part of these networks,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: known fancifully as the multi-layer perceptrons, or MLPs for short. In the last couple of chapters,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: you and I have been digging into the details behind transformers, the architecture underlying

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: large language models, and also underlying a lot of other modern AI. In the most recent chapter,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: we were focusing on a piece called attention. And the next step, for you and me,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: is to dig into the details of what happens inside these multi-layer perceptrons,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: which make up the other big portion of the network. The computation here is actually relatively

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: simple, especially when you compare it to attention. It boils down essentially to a pair

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: of matrix multiplications with a simple something in between. However, interpreting what these

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: computations are doing is exceedingly challenging. Our main goal here is to step through the computations

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and make them memorable, but I'd like to do it in the context of showing a specific example of how

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: one of these blocks could, at least in principle, store a concrete fact. Specifically, it'll be

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: storing the fact that Michael Jordan plays basketball. I should mention the layout here is inspired by a

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: conversation I had with one of those deep mind researchers, Neil Nanda. For the most part,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: I will assume that you've either watched the last two chapters, or otherwise you have a basic

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: sense for what a transformer is. But refreshers never hurt, so here's the quick reminder of the

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: overall flow. You and I have been studying a model that's trained to take in a piece of text

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and predict what comes next. That input text is first broken into a bunch of tokens, which means

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: little chunks that are typically words or little pieces of words. And each token is associated with a

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: high-dimensional vector, which is to say a long list of numbers. This sequence of vectors,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: then repeatedly passes through two kinds of operation. Attention, which allows the vectors

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: to pass information between one another, and then the multi-layer perceptrons, the thing that we're going

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: to dig into today. And also there's a certain normalization step in between. After this sequence of

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: vectors has flowed through many, many different iterations of both of these blocks, by the end, the hope is

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: that each vector has soaked up enough information, both from the context, all of the other words in the

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: input, and also from the general knowledge that was baked into the model weights through training,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: that it can be used to make a prediction of what token comes next. One of the key ideas that I want

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: you to have in your mind is that all of these vectors live in a very, very high-dimensional space,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and when you think about that space, different directions can encode different kinds of meaning.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: So a very classic example that I like to refer back to is how if you look at the embedding of woman,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and subtract the embedding of man, and you take that little step, and you add it to another masculine

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: noun, something like uncle, you land somewhere very, very close to the corresponding feminine noun.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In this sense, this particular direction encodes gender information. The idea is that many other

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: distinct directions in this super high-dimensional space could correspond to other features that the

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: model might want to represent. In a transformer, these vectors don't merely encode the meaning of a

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: single word, though. As they flow through the network, they imbibe a much richer meaning based on all the

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: context around them, and also based on the model's knowledge. Ultimately, each one needs to encode something

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: far, far beyond the meaning of a single word, since it needs to be sufficient to predict what will come next.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: We've already seen how attention blocks let you incorporate context, but a majority of the model

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: parameters actually live inside the MLP blocks, and one thought for what they might be doing is that

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: they offer extra capacity to store facts. Like I said, the lesson here is going to center on the

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: concrete toy example of how exactly it could store the fact that Michael Jordan plays basketball.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Now this toy example is going to require that you and I make a couple of assumptions about that

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: high-dimensional space. First, we'll suppose that one of the directions represents the idea of a first

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: name Michael, and then another nearly perpendicular direction represents the idea of the last name

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Jordan, and then yet a third direction will represent the idea of basketball. So specifically what I mean by

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: this is if you look in the network and you pluck out one of the vectors being processed, if its dot product

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: with this first name Michael direction is 1, that's what it would mean for the vector to be encoding

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: the idea of a person with that first name. Otherwise, that dot product would be 0 or negative, meaning the

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: vector doesn't really align with that direction. And for simplicity, let's completely ignore the very

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: reasonable question of what it might mean if that dot product was bigger than 1. Similarly, its dot

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: product with these other directions would tell you whether it represents the last name Jordan, or

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: basketball. So let's say a vector is meant to represent the full name Michael Jordan, then its dot

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: product with both of these directions would have to be 1. Since the text Michael Jordan spans two

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: different tokens, this would also mean we have to assume that an earlier attention block has successfully

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: passed information to the second of these two vectors, so as to ensure that it can encode both names.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: With all of those as the assumptions, let's now dive into the meat of the lesson. What happens inside a

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: multilayer perceptron? You might think of this sequence of vectors flowing into the block, and

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: remember, each vector was originally associated with one of the tokens from the input text. What's going to

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: happen is that each individual vector from that sequence goes through a short series of operations,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: we'll unpack them in just a moment, and at the end we'll get another vector with the same dimension.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: That other vector is going to get added to the original one that flowed in, and that sum is the result flowing out.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: This sequence of operations is something you apply to every vector in the sequence, associated with every

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: token in the input, and it all happens in parallel. In particular, the vectors don't talk to each other in this step,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: they're all kind of doing their own thing. And for you and me, that actually makes it a lot simpler, because it means if we

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: understand what happens to just one of the vectors through this block, we effectively understand what happens to all of them.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: When I say this block is going to encode the fact that Michael Jordan plays basketball, what I mean is that if a vector

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: flows in that encodes first name Michael and last name Jordan, then this sequence of computations will produce something that

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: includes that direction basketball, which is what will add on to the vector in that position.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The first step of this process looks like multiplying that vector by a very big matrix.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: No surprises there, this is deep learning. And this matrix, like all of the other ones we've seen, is filled with model

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: parameters that are learned from data, which you might think of as a bunch of knobs and dials that get tweaked and

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: tuned to determine what the model behavior is. Now one nice way to think about matrix multiplication is to imagine each row of that matrix as being its own vector, and taking a bunch of dot products between those rows

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and the vector being processed, which I'll label as e for embedding.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: For example, suppose that very first row happened to equal this first name Michael direction that we're presuming exists.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: That would mean that the first component in this output, this dot product right here, would be 1 if that vector encodes the first name Michael, and 0 or negative otherwise.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Even more fun, take a moment to think about what it would mean if that first row was this first name Michael plus last name Jordan direction.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And for simplicity, let me go ahead and write that down as m plus j.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Then taking a dot product with this embedding e, things distribute really nicely, so it looks like m dot e plus j dot e.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And notice how that means the ultimate value would be 2 if the vector encodes the full name Michael Jordan, and otherwise it would be 1 or something smaller than 1.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And that's just one row in this matrix. You might think of all of the other rows as in parallel asking some other kinds of questions, probing at some other sorts of features of the vector being processed.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Very often this step also involves adding another vector to the output which is full of model parameters learned from data. This other vector is known as the bias.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: For our example, I want you to imagine that the value of this bias in that very first component is negative 1, meaning our final output looks like that relevant dot product but minus 1.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: You might very reasonably ask why I would want you to assume that the model has learned this, and in a moment you'll see why it's very clean and nice if we have a value here which is positive if and only if our vector encodes the full name Michael Jordan, and otherwise it's 0 or negative.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The total number of rows in this matrix, which is something like the number of questions being asked in the case of GPT-3, whose numbers we've been following, is just under 50,000.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In fact, it's exactly four times the number of dimensions in this embedding space.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: That's a design choice, you could make it more, you could make it less, but having a clean multiple tends to be friendly for hardware.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Since this matrix full of weights maps us into a higher dimensional space, I'm going to give it the shorthand w up.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: I'll continue labeling the vector we're processing as e, and let's label this bias vector as b up and put that all back down in the diagram.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: At this point, a problem is that this operation is purely linear, but language is a very non-linear process.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: If the entry that we're measuring is high for Michael plus Jordan, it would also necessarily be somewhat triggered by Michael plus Phelps, and also Alexis plus Jordan, despite those being unrelated conceptually.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: What you really want is a simple yes or no for the full name.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: So the next step is to pass this large intermediate vector through a very simple non-linear function.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: A common choice is one that takes all of the negative values and maps them to zero, and leaves all of the positive values unchanged.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And continuing with the deep learning tradition of overly fancy names, this very simple function is often called the Rectified Linear Unit, or RELU for short.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Here's what the graph looks like.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: So taking our imagined example where this first entry of the intermediate vector is one, if and only if the full name is Michael Jordan, and zero or negative otherwise,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: after you pass it through the RELU, you end up with a very clean value where all of the zero and negative values just get clipped to zero.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: So this output would be one for the full name Michael Jordan and zero otherwise.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In other words, it very directly mimics the behavior of an AND gate.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Often models will use a slightly modified function that's called the JLU, which has the same basic shape but it's just a bit smoother.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: But for our purposes, it's a little bit cleaner if we only think about the RELU.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Also, when you hear people refer to the neurons of a transformer, they're talking about these values right here.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Whenever you see that common neural network picture with a layer of dots and a bunch of lines connecting to the previous layer,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: which we had earlier in this series, that's typically meant to convey this combination of a linear step, a matrix multiplication,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: followed by some simple term-wise non-linear function like a RELU.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: You would say that this neuron is active whenever this value is positive, and that it's inactive if that value is zero.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The next step looks very similar to the first one.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: You multiply by a very large matrix and you add on a certain bias term.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In this case, the number of dimensions in the output is back down to the size of that embedding space,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: so I'm going to go ahead and call this the down projection matrix.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And this time, instead of thinking of things row by row, it's actually nicer to think of it column by column.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: You see, another way that you can hold matrix multiplication in your head is to imagine taking each column of the matrix

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and multiplying it by the corresponding term in the vector that it's processing and adding together all of those rescaled columns.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The reason it's nicer to think about this way is because here the columns have the same dimension as the embedding space,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: so we can think of them as directions in that space.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: For instance, we will imagine that the model has learned to make that first column into this basketball direction that we suppose exists.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: What that would mean is that when the relevant neuron in that first position is active,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: we'll be adding this column to the final result.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: But if that neuron was inactive, if that number was zero, then this would have no effect.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And it doesn't just have to be basketball.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The model could also bake into this column many other features that it wants to associate with something that has the full name Michael Jordan.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And at the same time, all of the other columns in this matrix are telling you what will be added to the final result if the corresponding neuron is active.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And if you have a bias in this case, it's something that you're just adding every single time, regardless of the neuron values.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: You might wonder what's that doing.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: As with all parameter-filled objects here, it's kind of hard to say exactly.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Maybe there's some bookkeeping that the network needs to do, but you can feel free to ignore it for now.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Making our notation a little more compact again, I'll call this big matrix w down,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and similarly call that bias vector b down, and put that back into our diagram.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Like I previewed earlier, what you do with this final result is add it to the vector that flowed into the block at that position,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and that gets you this final result.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: So for example, if the vector flowing in encoded both first name Michael and last name Jordan,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: then because this sequence of operations will trigger that AND gate, it will add ON the basketball direction,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: so what pops out will encode all of those together.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And remember, this is a process happening to every one of those vectors in parallel.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In particular, taking the GPT-3 numbers, it means that this block doesn't just have 50,000 neurons in it,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: it has 50,000 times the number of tokens in the input.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: So that is the entire operation.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Two matrix products, each with a bias added, and a simple clipping function in between.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Any of you who watched the earlier videos of the series will recognize this structure as the most basic kind of neural network that we studied there.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In that example, it was trained to recognize handwritten digits.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Over here, in the context of a transformer for a large language model, this is one piece in a larger architecture,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and any attempt to interpret what exactly it's doing is heavily intertwined with the idea of encoding information into vectors of a high-dimensional embedding space.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: That is the core lesson, but I do want to step back and reflect on two different things.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The first of which is a kind of bookkeeping, and the second of which involves a very thought-provoking fact about higher dimensions that I actually didn't know until I dug into Transformers.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In the last two chapters, you and I started counting up the total number of parameters in GPT-3, and seeing exactly where they live, so let's quickly finish up the game here.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: I already mentioned how this up-projection matrix has just under 50,000 rows, and that each row matches the size of the embedding space, which for GPT-3 is 12,288.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Multiplying those together, it gives us 604 million parameters just for that matrix.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And the down-projection has the same number of parameters, just with a transposed shape.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: So together, they give about 1.2 billion parameters.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The bias vector also accounts for a couple more parameters, but it's a trivial proportion of the total, so I'm not even going to show it.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In GPT-3, this sequence of embedding vectors flows through not one, but 96 distinct MLPs, so the total number of parameters devoted to all of these blocks adds up to about 116 billion.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: This is around two-thirds of the total parameters in the network, and when you add it to everything that we had before, for the attention blocks, the embedding, and the unembedding, you do indeed get that grand total of 175 billion as advertised.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: It's probably worth mentioning there's another set of parameters associated with those normalization steps that this explanation has skipped over,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: but like the bias vector, they account for a very trivial proportion of the total.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: As to that second point of reflection, you might be wondering if this central toy example we've been spending so much time on reflects how facts are actually stored in real large language models.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: It is true that the rows of that first matrix can be thought of as directions in this embedding space, and that means the activation of each neuron tells you how much a given vector aligns with some specific direction.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: It's also true that the columns of that second matrix tell you what will be added to the result if that neuron is active.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Both of those are just mathematical facts.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: However, the evidence does suggest that individual neurons very rarely represent a single clean feature like Michael Jordan.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And there may actually be a very good reason this is the case, related to an idea floating around interpretability researchers these days known as superposition.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: This is a hypothesis that might help to explain both why the models are especially hard to interpret, and also why they scale surprisingly well.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The basic idea is that if you have an n-dimensional space, and you want to represent a bunch of different features using directions that are all perpendicular to one another in that space,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: you know, that way if you add a component in one direction, it doesn't influence any of the other directions,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: then the maximum number of vectors you can fit is only n, the number of dimensions.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: To a mathematician, actually, this is the definition of dimension.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: But where it gets interesting is if you relax that constraint a little bit, and you tolerate some noise.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Say you allow those features to be represented by vectors that aren't exactly perpendicular, they're just nearly perpendicular, maybe between 89 and 91 degrees apart.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: If we were in two or three dimensions, this makes no difference.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: That gives you hardly any extra wiggle room to fit more vectors in.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Which makes it all the more counterintuitive that for higher dimensions, the answer changes dramatically.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: I can give you a really quick and dirty illustration of this using some scrappy python that's going to create a list of 100 dimensional vectors, each one initialized randomly.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And this list is going to contain 10,000 distinct vectors, so 100 times as many vectors as there are dimensions.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: This plot right here shows the distribution of angles between pairs of these vectors.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: So, because they started at random, those angles could be anything from 0 to 180 degrees.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: But you'll notice that already, even just for random vectors, there's this heavy bias for things to be closer to 90 degrees.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: Then what I'm going to do is run a certain optimization process that iteratively nudges all of these vectors so that they try to become more perpendicular to one another.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: After repeating this many different times, here's what the distribution of angles looks like.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: We have to actually zoom in on it here, because all of the possible angles between pairs of vectors sit inside this narrow range between 89 and 91 degrees.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: In general, a consequence of something known as the Johnson-Lindenstrauss Lemma is that the number of vectors you can cram into a space that are nearly perpendicular like this grows exponentially with the number of dimensions.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: This is very significant for large language models, which might benefit from associating independent ideas with nearly perpendicular directions.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: It means that it's possible for it to store many, many more ideas than there are dimensions in the space that it's allotted.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: This might partially explain why model performance seems to scale so well with size.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: A space that has 10 times as many dimensions can store way, way more than 10 times as many independent ideas.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: And this is relevant not just to that embedding space where the vectors flowing through the model live, but also to that vector full of neurons in the middle of that multilayer perceptron that we just studied.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: That is to say, at the sizes of GPT-3, it might not just be probing at 50,000 features, but if it instead leveraged this enormous added capacity by using nearly perpendicular directions of the space,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: it could be probing at many, many more features of the vector being processed.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: But if it was doing that, what it means is that individual features aren't going to be visible as a single neuron lighting up.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: It would have to look like some specific combination of neurons instead, a superposition.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: For any of you curious to learn more, a key relevant search term here is sparse autoencoder,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: which is a tool that some of the interpretability people use to try to extract what the true features are even if they're very superimposed on all these neurons.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: I'll link to a couple really great anthropic posts all about this.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: At this point, we haven't touched every detail of a transformer, but you and I have hit the most important points.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: The main thing that I want to cover in a next chapter is the training process.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: On the one hand, the short answer for how training works is that it's all backpropagation,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: and we covered backpropagation in a separate context with earlier chapters in the series.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: But there is more to discuss, like the specific cost function used for language models,

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: the idea of fine-tuning using reinforcement learning with human feedback, and the notion of scaling laws.

chunks.title: How might LLMs store facts Deep Learning
chunks.text: so it might be a while, but I do promise it'll come in due time.

text: If you feed a large language model the phrase, Michael Jordan plays the sport of blank, and you have it predict what comes next, and it correctly predicts basketball, this would suggest that somewhere inside its hundreds of billions of parameters, it's baked in knowledge about a specific person and his specific sport. And I think in general, anyone who's played around with one of these models has the clear sense that it's memorized tons and tons of facts. So a reasonable question you could ask is, how exactly does that work? And where do those facts live? Last December, a few researchers from Google DeepMind posted about work on this question, and they were using this specific example of matching athletes to their sports. And although a full mechanistic understanding of how facts are stored remains unsolved, they had some interesting partial results, including the very general high-level conclusion that the facts seem to live inside a specific part of these networks, known fancifully as the multi-layer perceptrons, or MLPs for short. In the last couple of chapters, you and I have been digging into the details behind transformers, the architecture underlying large language models, and also underlying a lot of other modern AI. In the most recent chapter, we were focusing on a piece called attention. And the next step, for you and me, is to dig into the details of what happens inside these multi-layer perceptrons, which make up the other big portion of the network. The computation here is actually relatively simple, especially when you compare it to attention. It boils down essentially to a pair of matrix multiplications with a simple something in between. However, interpreting what these computations are doing is exceedingly challenging. Our main goal here is to step through the computations and make them memorable, but I'd like to do it in the context of showing a specific example of how one of these blocks could, at least in principle, store a concrete fact. Specifically, it'll be storing the fact that Michael Jordan plays basketball. I should mention the layout here is inspired by a conversation I had with one of those deep mind researchers, Neil Nanda. For the most part, I will assume that you've either watched the last two chapters, or otherwise you have a basic sense for what a transformer is. But refreshers never hurt, so here's the quick reminder of the overall flow. You and I have been studying a model that's trained to take in a piece of text and predict what comes next. That input text is first broken into a bunch of tokens, which means little chunks that are typically words or little pieces of words. And each token is associated with a high-dimensional vector, which is to say a long list of numbers. This sequence of vectors, then repeatedly passes through two kinds of operation. Attention, which allows the vectors to pass information between one another, and then the multi-layer perceptrons, the thing that we're going to dig into today. And also there's a certain normalization step in between. After this sequence of vectors has flowed through many, many different iterations of both of these blocks, by the end, the hope is that each vector has soaked up enough information, both from the context, all of the other words in the input, and also from the general knowledge that was baked into the model weights through training, that it can be used to make a prediction of what token comes next. One of the key ideas that I want you to have in your mind is that all of these vectors live in a very, very high-dimensional space, and when you think about that space, different directions can encode different kinds of meaning. So a very classic example that I like to refer back to is how if you look at the embedding of woman, and subtract the embedding of man, and you take that little step, and you add it to another masculine noun, something like uncle, you land somewhere very, very close to the corresponding feminine noun. In this sense, this particular direction encodes gender information. The idea is that many other distinct directions in this super high-dimensional space could correspond to other features that the model might want to represent. In a transformer, these vectors don't merely encode the meaning of a single word, though. As they flow through the network, they imbibe a much richer meaning based on all the context around them, and also based on the model's knowledge. Ultimately, each one needs to encode something far, far beyond the meaning of a single word, since it needs to be sufficient to predict what will come next. We've already seen how attention blocks let you incorporate context, but a majority of the model parameters actually live inside the MLP blocks, and one thought for what they might be doing is that they offer extra capacity to store facts. Like I said, the lesson here is going to center on the concrete toy example of how exactly it could store the fact that Michael Jordan plays basketball. Now this toy example is going to require that you and I make a couple of assumptions about that high-dimensional space. First, we'll suppose that one of the directions represents the idea of a first name Michael, and then another nearly perpendicular direction represents the idea of the last name Jordan, and then yet a third direction will represent the idea of basketball. So specifically what I mean by this is if you look in the network and you pluck out one of the vectors being processed, if its dot product with this first name Michael direction is 1, that's what it would mean for the vector to be encoding the idea of a person with that first name. Otherwise, that dot product would be 0 or negative, meaning the vector doesn't really align with that direction. And for simplicity, let's completely ignore the very reasonable question of what it might mean if that dot product was bigger than 1. Similarly, its dot product with these other directions would tell you whether it represents the last name Jordan, or basketball. So let's say a vector is meant to represent the full name Michael Jordan, then its dot product with both of these directions would have to be 1. Since the text Michael Jordan spans two different tokens, this would also mean we have to assume that an earlier attention block has successfully passed information to the second of these two vectors, so as to ensure that it can encode both names. With all of those as the assumptions, let's now dive into the meat of the lesson. What happens inside a multilayer perceptron? You might think of this sequence of vectors flowing into the block, and remember, each vector was originally associated with one of the tokens from the input text. What's going to happen is that each individual vector from that sequence goes through a short series of operations, we'll unpack them in just a moment, and at the end we'll get another vector with the same dimension. That other vector is going to get added to the original one that flowed in, and that sum is the result flowing out. This sequence of operations is something you apply to every vector in the sequence, associated with every token in the input, and it all happens in parallel. In particular, the vectors don't talk to each other in this step, they're all kind of doing their own thing. And for you and me, that actually makes it a lot simpler, because it means if we understand what happens to just one of the vectors through this block, we effectively understand what happens to all of them. When I say this block is going to encode the fact that Michael Jordan plays basketball, what I mean is that if a vector flows in that encodes first name Michael and last name Jordan, then this sequence of computations will produce something that includes that direction basketball, which is what will add on to the vector in that position. The first step of this process looks like multiplying that vector by a very big matrix. No surprises there, this is deep learning. And this matrix, like all of the other ones we've seen, is filled with model parameters that are learned from data, which you might think of as a bunch of knobs and dials that get tweaked and tuned to determine what the model behavior is. Now one nice way to think about matrix multiplication is to imagine each row of that matrix as being its own vector, and taking a bunch of dot products between those rows and the vector being processed, which I'll label as e for embedding. For example, suppose that very first row happened to equal this first name Michael direction that we're presuming exists. That would mean that the first component in this output, this dot product right here, would be 1 if that vector encodes the first name Michael, and 0 or negative otherwise. Even more fun, take a moment to think about what it would mean if that first row was this first name Michael plus last name Jordan direction. And for simplicity, let me go ahead and write that down as m plus j. Then taking a dot product with this embedding e, things distribute really nicely, so it looks like m dot e plus j dot e. And notice how that means the ultimate value would be 2 if the vector encodes the full name Michael Jordan, and otherwise it would be 1 or something smaller than 1. And that's just one row in this matrix. You might think of all of the other rows as in parallel asking some other kinds of questions, probing at some other sorts of features of the vector being processed. Very often this step also involves adding another vector to the output which is full of model parameters learned from data. This other vector is known as the bias. For our example, I want you to imagine that the value of this bias in that very first component is negative 1, meaning our final output looks like that relevant dot product but minus 1. You might very reasonably ask why I would want you to assume that the model has learned this, and in a moment you'll see why it's very clean and nice if we have a value here which is positive if and only if our vector encodes the full name Michael Jordan, and otherwise it's 0 or negative. The total number of rows in this matrix, which is something like the number of questions being asked in the case of GPT-3, whose numbers we've been following, is just under 50,000. In fact, it's exactly four times the number of dimensions in this embedding space. That's a design choice, you could make it more, you could make it less, but having a clean multiple tends to be friendly for hardware. Since this matrix full of weights maps us into a higher dimensional space, I'm going to give it the shorthand w up. I'll continue labeling the vector we're processing as e, and let's label this bias vector as b up and put that all back down in the diagram. At this point, a problem is that this operation is purely linear, but language is a very non-linear process. If the entry that we're measuring is high for Michael plus Jordan, it would also necessarily be somewhat triggered by Michael plus Phelps, and also Alexis plus Jordan, despite those being unrelated conceptually. What you really want is a simple yes or no for the full name. So the next step is to pass this large intermediate vector through a very simple non-linear function. A common choice is one that takes all of the negative values and maps them to zero, and leaves all of the positive values unchanged. And continuing with the deep learning tradition of overly fancy names, this very simple function is often called the Rectified Linear Unit, or RELU for short. Here's what the graph looks like. So taking our imagined example where this first entry of the intermediate vector is one, if and only if the full name is Michael Jordan, and zero or negative otherwise, after you pass it through the RELU, you end up with a very clean value where all of the zero and negative values just get clipped to zero. So this output would be one for the full name Michael Jordan and zero otherwise. In other words, it very directly mimics the behavior of an AND gate. Often models will use a slightly modified function that's called the JLU, which has the same basic shape but it's just a bit smoother. But for our purposes, it's a little bit cleaner if we only think about the RELU. Also, when you hear people refer to the neurons of a transformer, they're talking about these values right here. Whenever you see that common neural network picture with a layer of dots and a bunch of lines connecting to the previous layer, which we had earlier in this series, that's typically meant to convey this combination of a linear step, a matrix multiplication, followed by some simple term-wise non-linear function like a RELU. You would say that this neuron is active whenever this value is positive, and that it's inactive if that value is zero. The next step looks very similar to the first one. You multiply by a very large matrix and you add on a certain bias term. In this case, the number of dimensions in the output is back down to the size of that embedding space, so I'm going to go ahead and call this the down projection matrix. And this time, instead of thinking of things row by row, it's actually nicer to think of it column by column. You see, another way that you can hold matrix multiplication in your head is to imagine taking each column of the matrix and multiplying it by the corresponding term in the vector that it's processing and adding together all of those rescaled columns. The reason it's nicer to think about this way is because here the columns have the same dimension as the embedding space, so we can think of them as directions in that space. For instance, we will imagine that the model has learned to make that first column into this basketball direction that we suppose exists. What that would mean is that when the relevant neuron in that first position is active, we'll be adding this column to the final result. But if that neuron was inactive, if that number was zero, then this would have no effect. And it doesn't just have to be basketball. The model could also bake into this column many other features that it wants to associate with something that has the full name Michael Jordan. And at the same time, all of the other columns in this matrix are telling you what will be added to the final result if the corresponding neuron is active. And if you have a bias in this case, it's something that you're just adding every single time, regardless of the neuron values. You might wonder what's that doing. As with all parameter-filled objects here, it's kind of hard to say exactly. Maybe there's some bookkeeping that the network needs to do, but you can feel free to ignore it for now. Making our notation a little more compact again, I'll call this big matrix w down, and similarly call that bias vector b down, and put that back into our diagram. Like I previewed earlier, what you do with this final result is add it to the vector that flowed into the block at that position, and that gets you this final result. So for example, if the vector flowing in encoded both first name Michael and last name Jordan, then because this sequence of operations will trigger that AND gate, it will add ON the basketball direction, so what pops out will encode all of those together. And remember, this is a process happening to every one of those vectors in parallel. In particular, taking the GPT-3 numbers, it means that this block doesn't just have 50,000 neurons in it, it has 50,000 times the number of tokens in the input. So that is the entire operation. In that example, it was trained to recognize handwritten digits. Over here, in the context of a transformer for a large language model, this is one piece in a larger architecture, and any attempt to interpret what exactly it's doing is heavily intertwined with the idea of encoding information into vectors of a high-dimensional embedding space. That is the core lesson, but I do want to step back and reflect on two different things. The first of which is a kind of bookkeeping, and the second of which involves a very thought-provoking fact about higher dimensions that I actually didn't know until I dug into Transformers. In the last two chapters, you and I started counting up the total number of parameters in GPT-3, and seeing exactly where they live, so let's quickly finish up the game here. I already mentioned how this up-projection matrix has just under 50,000 rows, and that each row matches the size of the embedding space, which for GPT-3 is 12,288. Multiplying those together, it gives us 604 million parameters just for that matrix. And the down-projection has the same number of parameters, just with a transposed shape. So together, they give about 1.2 billion parameters. The bias vector also accounts for a couple more parameters, but it's a trivial proportion of the total, so I'm not even going to show it. In GPT-3, this sequence of embedding vectors flows through not one, but 96 distinct MLPs, so the total number of parameters devoted to all of these blocks adds up to about 116 billion. This is around two-thirds of the total parameters in the network, and when you add it to everything that we had before, for the attention blocks, the embedding, and the unembedding, you do indeed get that grand total of 175 billion as advertised. It's probably worth mentioning there's another set of parameters associated with those normalization steps that this explanation has skipped over, but like the bias vector, they account for a very trivial proportion of the total. As to that second point of reflection, you might be wondering if this central toy example we've been spending so much time on reflects how facts are actually stored in real large language models. It is true that the rows of that first matrix can be thought of as directions in this embedding space, and that means the activation of each neuron tells you how much a given vector aligns with some specific direction. It's also true that the columns of that second matrix tell you what will be added to the result if that neuron is active. Both of those are just mathematical facts. However, the evidence does suggest that individual neurons very rarely represent a single clean feature like Michael Jordan. And there may actually be a very good reason this is the case, related to an idea floating around interpretability researchers these days known as superposition. This is a hypothesis that might help to explain both why the models are especially hard to interpret, and also why they scale surprisingly well. The basic idea is that if you have an n-dimensional space, and you want to represent a bunch of different features using directions that are all perpendicular to one another in that space, you know, that way if you add a component in one direction, it doesn't influence any of the other directions, then the maximum number of vectors you can fit is only n, the number of dimensions. To a mathematician, actually, this is the definition of dimension. But where it gets interesting is if you relax that constraint a little bit, and you tolerate some noise. Say you allow those features to be represented by vectors that aren't exactly perpendicular, they're just nearly perpendicular, maybe between 89 and 91 degrees apart. If we were in two or three dimensions, this makes no difference. That gives you hardly any extra wiggle room to fit more vectors in. Which makes it all the more counterintuitive that for higher dimensions, the answer changes dramatically.
